concepts.title: Straight line
concepts.conceptBody:
Straight Lines

Definition: A straight line defined as the curve which is such that the line segment joining any two points on it lies wholly on it.

Note: 

  1. The number of arbitrary constants in the equation of a straight line is two (we observe three constants $\text{a},\text{b}$ and $\text{c}$ in the equation $\text{ax}+\text{by}+\text{c}=0$ of a straight line. The given equation of line can be rewritten as $\left( \frac{a}{c} \right)x+\left( \frac{b}{c} \right)y+1=0$ or $px+qy+1=0$ where $p=\frac{a}{c}$ and $q=\frac{b}{c}$.

Thus, we have only two arbitrary constants $\text{p}$ and $\text{q}$ in the equation of a straight line).

Hence to completely determine the equation of a straight line, we require two conditions to determine the two unknowns in general.

  1. A straight line is briefly written as a 'line.'
  2. The equation of a straight line is the relation between $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}$, which is satisfied by the coordinates of each and every point on the line.

The inclination of a line is defined to be the smallest counter-clockwise angle through which the positively directed $\text{x}$-axis must turn in order to coincide with the line. This angle is always measured from the x-axis to the line. In both Fig., angle $\alpha $ is the inclination of the line. The inclination of any horizontal line is defined to be 0. The inclination is always less than 180. 
 

The tangent of the inclination of a line is called the slope of the line. We shall represent slope by $\text{m}$. If the line makes an acute angle with the positive x-axis, the slope is positive; if the line makes an obtuse angle with the positive x-axis, the slope is negative; if the line is parallel to the 
 

$\text{x}$-axis, the slope is 0; and, if the inclination is 90, the slope is not defined. Conversely, if $\text{s}$ is positive, the inclination is an acute angle; if $\text{s}$ is negative, the inclination is an obtuse angle; if $\text{m}=0$, the inclination is 0 ; if $\text{s}$ is undefined, the inclination is 90 .

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ slope $=\text{m}=\text{tan}\alpha $

Q) Find the slope of a line that passes through the points ${{P}_{1}}=\left( -3,4 \right)$ and ${{P}_{2}}=\left( 5,-1 \right)$. 

Solution: We know that ${{\text{\vec{P}}}_{1}}{{\text{\vec{P}}}_{2}}=\left( 8,-5 \right)$; therefore, $\text{s}=-5/8$. The student should notice that, if we had taken $\overrightarrow{{{\text{P}}_{2}}{{\text{P}}_{1}}}=\left[ -8,5 \right]$, we would also have obtained $\text{s}=-\frac{5}{8}$.

Note: 

  1. Slope of a line is not the angle but is the tangent of the inclination of the line.
  2. If a line is parallel to $\text{x}$-axis then its slope $=\text{tan}0=0$.
  3. Slope of a line parallel to y-axis or perpendicular to x-axis is not defined. Whenever we say that the slope of a line is not defined.
  4. If a line is equally inclined to the axes, then it will make an angle of 45 or 135 with the positive direction of $\text{x}$-axis. Slope in this case will be $\text{tan}45$ or $\text{tan}135$ i.e., \pm 1 . 
     

  1. When the two lines are parallel, then their slopes are equal i.e., ${{\text{m}}_{1}}={{\text{m}}_{2}}$.
  2. If three points $\text{A},\text{B},\text{C}$ are collinear, then slope of $\text{AB}=$ slope of $\text{BC}=$ slope of $\text{AC}$

Intercepts of a line on axes 

If a line cuts x-axis at $\text{A}\left( \text{a},0 \right)$ and the $\text{y}$-axis at $\text{B}\left( 0,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b} \right)$ then $\text{OA}$ and $\text{OB}$ are known as the intercepts of the line on $\text{x}$-axis and $\text{y}$-axis respectively. $\left| \text{a} \right|$ is called the length of intercept of the line on x-axis. Intercept of a line on x-axis may be positive or negative and $\left| b \right|$ is called the length of intercept of the line on y-axis. Intercept of a line on y-axis may be positive or negative.

Remark: If a line parallel to y-axis, then its intercept on y-axis is not defined and if a line parallel to $\text{x}$-axis, then its intercept on $\text{x}$-axis is not defined.

concepts.title: Area of triangle
concepts.conceptBody:
Area of Triangle 

To find the area of the triangle, the coordinates of whose angular points are given, the axes being rectangular.

Let $\text{ABC}$ be the triangle and let the coordinates of its angular points $\text{A},\text{B}$ and $\text{C}$ be $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right),\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}} \right.$, $\left. {{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ and $\left( {{\text{x}}_{3}},{{\text{y}}_{3}} \right)$.

Draw $\text{AL},\text{BM}$ and $\text{CN}$ perpendicular to the axis of $\text{x}$, and let $\text{Y}$ $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }$ denote the required area.

Then,

$\Delta=\text{ trapezium }\text{ ALNC }\text{ + }\text{ trapezium }$ CNMB - trapezium ALMB

$=\frac{1}{2}\text{LN}\left( \text{LA}+\text{NC} \right)+\frac{1}{2}\text{NM}\left( \text{NC}+\text{MB} \right) -\frac{1}{2}\text{LM}\left( \text{LA}+\text{MB} \right)$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \left( {{\text{x}}_{3}}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\left( {{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{y}}_{3}} \right)+\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{3}} \right)\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}+{{\text{y}}_{3}} \right)-\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\left( {{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right) \right]$

On simplifying we easily have

$\Delta=\frac{1}{2}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{3}}-{{\text{x}}_{3}}{{\text{y}}_{1}}-{{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{3}} \right)$

or the equivalent form,

$\Delta=\left[ {{\text{x}}_{1}}\left( {{\text{y}}_{2}}-{{\text{y}}_{3}} \right)+{{\text{x}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{y}}_{3}}-{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)+{{\text{x}}_{3}}\left( {{\text{y}}_{1}}-{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right) \right.$

If we use the determinant notation this may be written as

$\Delta=\frac{1}{2}\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{x}}_{1}}, & {{\text{y}}_{1}}, & 1 \\ {{\text{x}}_{2}}, & {{\text{y}}_{2}}, & 1 \\ {{\text{x}}_{3}} & {{\text{y}}_{3}} & 1 \\ \end{array} \right|$

Cor: The area of the triangle whose vertices are the origin $\left( 0,0 \right)$ and the points $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$,

$\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)\text{ is }\frac{1}{2}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)\text{. }$

Note : In order that the expression for the area may be a positive quantity (as all areas necessarily are) the points $\text{A},\text{B}$ and $\text{C}$ must be taken in the order in which they would be met by a person starting from A and walking round the triangle in such a manner that the area of the triangle is always on his left hand. Otherwise above expression would be found to be negative. Thus in general to find the area of a quadrilateral the coordinates of whose angular points are given.

concepts.title: Area of Triangle [coordinate]
concepts.conceptBody:
Area of the triangle $\left( \Delta\right)$

The area of the triangle is a. h /2.

But in triangle $\text{BAH}$, we have $\text{sin}\left( \text{B} \right)=\text{h}/\text{c}$.

Hence the area of the triangle is a. c. $\text{sin}\left( \text{B} \right)/2$.

Similarly we have that the area of the triangle

$=$ b.c. $\text{sin}\left( \text{A} \right)/2=$ a $\cdot $ b. $\text{sin}\left( \text{C} \right)/2$

The area of a triangle $\text{ABC}=$ a.c. $\text{sin}\frac{\text{B}}{2}=$ b.c. $\text{sin}\frac{\text{A}}{2}=$ a.b. $\text{sin}\frac{\text{C}}{2}$

concepts.title: Rectangular Coordinate System
concepts.conceptBody:
The rectangular coordinate system

The old familiar rectangular coordinate system is just two directed lines meeting at right angles. The point of intersection is called the origin and is usually lettered $\text{O}$. It is customary to make one of these lines horizontal and to take the direction to the right of $\text{O}$ as the positive direction on this line. This horizontal line is called the $\text{x}$-axis, or the horizontal axis. The other-directed line which is perpendicular to the $\text{x}$-axis is called the $\text{y}$-axis, or the vertical axis, and the positive direction on this axis is upward from O. Q. I, Q. II, Q. III and Q. IV for convenience, as indicated in Fig.

Once a rectangular coordinate system has been chosen, any point in the plane can be located with respect to it. Suppose $\text{P}$ is some point in the plane. Let $\text{PQ}$ be the line segment from $\text{P}$ perpendicular to the $\text{x}$-axis at the point $\text{Q}$, and let $\text{PR}$ be the line segment perpendicular to the ${{\text{y}}}$ -axis at the point $R$ (see Fig. 2). Then the directed distance $OQ$ is called the ${x}$ -coordinate of $P$, or the abscissa of $\text{P}$. The directed distance $\text{OR}$ is called the $\text{y}$ -coordinate of $\text{P}$, or the ordinate of $\text{P}$. For example, in Fig., $\text{OQ}=1$ and $\text{OR}=3$, so the $\text{x}$-coordinate, the abscissa of $\text{P}$, is 1 and the $\text{y}$-coordinate, the ordinate of $\text{P}$, is 3.

It is customary to enclose this pair of numbers in parenthesis thus : $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ or in our specific case $\left( 1,3 \right)$ and these numbers are called the coordinates of the point P. Since the figure OQPR is a rectangle $\text{OQ}=\text{RP}$ and $\text{OR}=\text{QP}$, so an alternate definition is possible, namely :

The ${{\text{x}}^{-}}$coordinate of $\text{P}$ is the directed distance of $\text{P}$ from the ${{\text{y}}}$ -axis,

The coordinate of $\mathrm{P}$ is the directed distance of $\mathrm{P}$ from the $\mathrm{x}$-axis.

Note: 

  1. The ordinate of every point on $x$-axis is 0.
  2. The abscissa of every point on y-axis is 0.
  3. Equation of $\text{x}$-axis, $\text{y}=0$ and equation of $\text{y}$-axis, $\text{x}=0$.
  4. The abscissa and ordinate of the origin $\text{O}\left( 0,0 \right)$ are both zero.
  5. The abscissa and ordinate of a point are at perpendicular distance from y-axis and x-axis respectively.
  6. Table for conversion sign of co-ordinates:

concepts.title: Polar Coordinate System
concepts.conceptBody:
Polar Co-ordinates of a point 

If $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ OP}=\text{r}$ (radius vector)

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\angle \text{XOP}=\theta $ (vectoral angle)

Then the ordered pair of real numbers $\left( r,\theta \right)$ is called the polar co-ordinates of the point $\text{P}$.

Convention of sign of polar coordinates 

  1. $r$ may be positive or negative according as $\theta $ is measured in anticlockwise or clockwise direction. $\theta $ lies between $\pi $ to $\pi $ i.e., $-\pi <\theta \le \pi $. If it is greater than $\pi $, then we subtract $2\pi $ from it and if it is less than $-\pi $, then we add $2\pi $ to it. $I$ is also known as principal value of $P$.
  2. Always take $\theta $ in radian.

Illustration 1 

Draw the polar co-ordinate $\left( 3,\frac{5\pi }{4} \right)$ on the plane. 

Solution: 

here $\theta=\frac{5 \pi}{4}>\pi$

then $\theta-2 \pi=\frac{5 \pi}{4}-2 \pi=-\frac{3 \pi}{4}$

$\therefore$ $\left(3, \frac{5 \pi}{4}\right)$ is same $\left(3,-\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)$

concepts.title: Relation between rectangular and polar coordinate system
concepts.conceptBody:
Relation between Cartesian and Polar Coordinates 

Let $\text{P}$ be the point $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ with reference to rectangular axes $\text{OX},\text{OY}$ and the point $\left( \text{r},\theta \right)$ with reference to the pole $\text{O}$ and initial line $\text{OX}$. i.e., the $\text{X}$-axis.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ we have $\text{x}=\text{rcos}\theta ;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\text{rsin}\theta $

Hence $\text{tan}\theta =\frac{\text{y}}{\text{x}}$ and ${{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}={{\text{r}}^{2}}$

These relations enable us to change from one system of coordinates to the other.

$\therefore$ $\theta \quad=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right)$

if $\alpha=\tan ^{-1}\left|\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}\right|$

The values of $\theta $ in four quadrants

concepts.title: Distance Formula in Rectangular System
concepts.conceptBody:
Distance formula 

Let two points ${{\text{P}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{P}}_{2}}$ be given in the plane. If we are given the coordinates $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ and $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ of these points then it is quite easy to compute the distance between the two points. Indeed, if we draw a line segment joining the two points and then make this segment the hypotenuse of a right triangle ${{\text{P}}_{1}}\text{Q}{{\text{P}}_{2}}$ as indicated in by drawing suitable lines parallel to the axes, the distance ${{\text{P}}_{1}}{{\text{P}}_{2}}$ can be computed by the Pythagorean Theorem.

$\left| {{\text{P}}_{1}}{{\text{P}}_{2}} \right|=\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}}$

where $\text{a}$ is the length of the line segment ${{\text{P}}_{1}}\text{Q}$ and $\text{b}$ is the length of the line segment $\text{Q}{{\text{P}}_{2}}$. Now along the line ${{\text{P}}_{1}}\text{Q}$, the height above the x-axis does not change, so that $\text{a}=\left| \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x} \right|$. Similarly along the line $\text{P}{{\text{Q}}_{2}}$ the $\text{x}$-coordinate does not change, so that $\text{b}=\mid \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ yl}$. In the case pictured in Fig. 4 , both $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}$ and $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ y}$ are positive, but in other cases they may not be, so the absolute value signs are necessary.

The distance between the points ${{\text{P}}_{1}}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ and ${{\text{P}}_{2}}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ is given by

$\text{d}=\sqrt{{{(\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x})}^{2}}+{{(\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ y})}^{2}}}=\sqrt{{{\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)}^{2}}+{{\left( {{\text{y}}_{2}}-{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)}^{2}}}.$

given two points in the plane, the distance between them does not depend on the letter assigned, so that either could be called ${{\text{P}}_{1}}$ and the other one ${{\text{P}}_{2}}$. Thus, either logically, or by inspection of above equation, the quantity $\text{d}$ is unchanged if the subscripts 1 and 2 are interchanged.

concepts.title: Various formulae to calculate area of triangle
concepts.conceptBody:
Area of Triangle

If the co-ordinates of the vertices of the triangle are given in polar form i.e., $\text{A}\left( {{\text{r}}_{1}},{{\theta }_{1}} \right),\text{B}\left( {{\text{r}}_{2}},{{\theta }_{2}} \right)$, $\text{C}\left( {{\text{r}}_{3}},{{\theta }_{3}} \right)$

Then area of triangle

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\frac{1}{2}\left[ {{r}_{1}}{{r}_{2}}\text{sin}\left( {{\theta }_{1}}-{{\theta }_{2}} \right)+{{r}_{2}}{{r}_{3}}\text{sin}\left( {{\theta }_{2}}-{{\theta }_{3}} \right)+{{r}_{3}}{{r}_{1}}\text{sin}\left( {{\theta }_{3}}-{{\theta }_{1}} \right) \right] \\ {} & ~=\frac{1}{2}\left| \sum ~{{r}_{1}}{{r}_{2}}\text{sin}\left( {{\theta }_{1}}-{{\theta }_{2}} \right) \right| \\ \end{array}$

Another formula

If ${{\text{a}}_{1}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{1}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{1}}=0,{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{2}}=0$ and ${{\text{a}}_{3}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{3}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{3}}=0$ are the sides of a triangle then the area of the triangle is given by (without solving the vertices)

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }=\frac{1}{2\left| {{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{3}} \right|}{{\left| \begin{matrix} {{\text{a}}_{1}} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{1}} & {{\text{c}}_{1}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{2}} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{2}} & {{\text{c}}_{2}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{3}} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{3}} & {{\text{c}}_{3}} \\ \end{matrix} \right|}^{2}}$

where ${{\text{C}}_{1}},{{\text{C}}_{2}},{{\text{C}}_{3}}$ are the cofactors of ${{\text{c}}_{1}},{{\text{c}}_{2}},{{\text{c}}_{3}}$ in the determinant

Here $\mathrm{C}_1=\left|\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{a}_2 & \mathrm{~b}_2 \\ \mathrm{a}_3 & \mathrm{~b}_3\end{array}\right|=\left(\mathrm{a}_2 \mathrm{~b}_3-\mathrm{a}_3 \mathrm{~b}_2\right)$

$\mathrm{C}_2=\left|\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{a}_3 & \mathrm{~b}_3 \\ \mathrm{a}_1 & \mathrm{~b}_1\end{array}\right|=\left(\mathrm{a}_3 \mathrm{~b}_1-\mathrm{a}_1 \mathrm{~b}_3\right)$

and $\mathrm{C}_3=\left|\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{a}_1 & \mathrm{~b}_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_2 & \mathrm{~b}_2\end{array}\right|=\left(\mathrm{a}_1 \mathrm{~b}_2-\mathrm{a}_2 \mathrm{~b}_1\right)$

and $\left|\begin{array}{lll}\mathrm{a}_1 & \mathrm{~b}_1 & \mathrm{c}_1 \\ \mathrm{a}_2 & \mathrm{~b}_2 & \mathrm{c}_2 \\ \mathrm{a}_3 & \mathrm{~b}_3 & \mathrm{c}_3\end{array}\right|=\mathrm{c}_1 \mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{c}_2 \mathrm{C}_2+\mathrm{c}_3 \mathrm{C}_3$

Area of triangle $=\frac{{{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}^{2}}}{2\left| {{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}_{1}}{{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}_{2}}{{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}_{3}} \right|}$

where $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{a}_{1}} & {{b}_{1}} & {{c}_{1}} \\ {{a}_{2}} & {{b}_{2}} & {{c}_{2}} \\ {{a}_{3}} & {{b}_{3}} & {{c}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right|,{{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}_{1}}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{a}_{1}} & {{b}_{1}} \\ {{a}_{2}} & {{b}_{2}} \\ \end{array} \right|,{{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}_{2}}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{a}_{2}} & {{b}_{2}} \\ {{a}_{3}} & b3 \\ \end{array} \right|$ and ${{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }}_{3}}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{a}_{3}} & {{b}_{3}} \\ {{a}_{1}} & {{b}_{1}} \\ \end{array} \right|$

Another formula 

Area of the triangle formed by the lines of the form $y={{m}_{1}}x+{{c}_{1}},y={{m}_{2}}x+{{c}_{2}}$ and $y$ $={{\text{m}}_{3}}\text{x}+{{\text{c}}_{3}}$ is

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }=\frac{1}{2}\left| \frac{{{\left( {{c}_{2}}-{{c}_{3}} \right)}^{2}}}{\left( {{m}_{2}}-{{m}_{3}} \right)}+\frac{{{\left( {{c}_{3}}-{{c}_{1}} \right)}^{2}}}{\left( {{m}_{3}}-{{m}_{1}} \right)}+\frac{{{\left( {{c}_{1}}-{{c}_{2}} \right)}^{2}}}{\left( {{m}_{1}}+{{m}_{2}} \right)} \right|$

Note: 

  1. If area of a triangle is given then use \pm sign.
  2. The points A (, ), B (, ) and C (, ) are collinear then area of (∆ABC = 0).
  3. Four given points will be collinear, if area of the quadrilateral is zero.
  4. Area of the triangle formed by the points $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right),\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ and $\left( {{\text{x}}_{3}},{{\text{y}}_{3}} \right)$ is

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }=\frac{1}{2}||\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{x}_{1}}-{{x}_{3}} & {{x}_{2}}-{{x}_{3}} \\ {{y}_{1}}-{{y}_{3}} & {{y}_{2}}-{{y}_{3}} \\ \end{array}\mid $

     5. If one vertex $\left( {{\text{x}}_{3}},{{\text{y}}_{3}} \right)$ is at $\left( 0,0 \right)$ then

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }=\frac{1}{2}\left| {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{2}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right|$

concepts.title: Area of polygon
concepts.conceptBody:
Area of polygon 

Area of polygon whose vertices are $\left( {{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}} \right),\left( {{x}_{2}},{{y}_{2}} \right),\left( {{x}_{3}},{{y}_{3}} \right),\ldots .,\left( {{x}_{n}},{{y}_{n}} \right)$ is 

$\frac{1}{2}\left| \left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{x}_{1}} & {{y}_{1}} \\ {{x}_{2}} & {{y}_{2}} \\ \end{array}\left| + \right|\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{x}_{2}} & {{y}_{2}} \\ {{x}_{3}} & {{y}_{3}} \\ \end{array}\left| + \right|\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{x}_{3}} & {{y}_{3}} \\ {{x}_{4}} & {{y}_{4}} \\ \end{array}\left| +\ldots \ldots + \right|\begin{matrix} {{x}_{n}} & {{y}_{n}} \\ {{x}_{1}} & {{y}_{1}} \\ \end{matrix} \right| \right|$

concepts.title: Centroid and it's properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Centroid of a Triangle 

Definition: The point of intersection of the medians of a triangle is called the centroid of the triangle and it divides the median internally in the ratio $2:1.$

Or

The co-ordinates of the centroid of the triangle whose vertices are $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right),\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ and $\left( {{\text{x}}_{3}},{{\text{y}}_{3}} \right)$ are

$\left( \frac{{{x}_{1}}+{{x}_{2}}+{{x}_{3}}}{3},\frac{{{y}_{1}}+{{y}_{2}}+{{y}_{3}}}{3} \right)$

The medians of a triangle are concurrent.

Key points: 

1. Centroid of the triangle obtained by joining the middle points of the sides of a triangle is the same as the centroid of the original triangle.

Or

If $\left( {{a}_{1}},{{b}_{1}} \right),\left( {{a}_{2}},{{b}_{2}} \right)$ and $\left( {{a}_{3}},{{b}_{3}} \right)$ are the mid points of the sides of a triangle, then its centroid is given by

$\left( \frac{{{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{2}}+{{\text{a}}_{3}}}{3},\frac{{{\text{b}}_{1}}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}+{{\text{b}}_{3}}}{3} \right)$

2. If two vertices of a triangle are $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ and $\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$ and the co-ordinates of centroid are $(\alpha $, $\beta )$ then co-ordinates of the third vertex are

$\left( 3\alpha -{{\text{x}}_{1}}-{{\text{x}}_{2}},3\beta -{{\text{y}}_{1}}-{{\text{y}}_{2}} \right)$

3. According to important theorem $\vartriangle \text{sABC}$ and $\text{DEF}$ are similar

$\therefore \frac{\text { Area of } \triangle \mathrm{ABC}}{\text { Area of } \triangle \mathrm{DEF}}=\frac{(\mathrm{BC})^2}{(\mathrm{EF})^2}$

$=\frac{4\left\{\left(\mathrm{a}_2-\mathrm{a}_3\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{b}_2-\mathrm{b}_3\right)^2\right\}}{\left\{\left(\mathrm{a}_2-\mathrm{a}_3\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{b}_2-\mathrm{b}_3\right)^2\right\}}=4$

concepts.title: Incenter and it's properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Incentre 

Definition: The point of intersection of internal angle bisectors of triangle is called the incentre of the triangle.

The co-ordinates of the incentre of a triangle whose vertices are $A\left( {{x}_{1}},{{y}_{1}} \right),B\left( {{x}_{2}},{{y}_{2}} \right),C\left( {{x}_{3}},{{y}_{3}} \right)$ are $\left( \frac{a{{x}_{1}}+b{{x}_{2}}+c{{x}_{3}}}{a+b+c},\frac{a{{y}_{1}}+b{{y}_{2}}+c{{y}_{3}}}{a+b+c} \right)$

where $\text{a},\text{b},\text{c}$ are the lengths of sides $\text{BC},\text{CA}$ and $\text{AB}$ respectively.

Note: If ∆ABC is equilateral then $\text{a}=\text{b}=\text{c}$

Then $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ incentre }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\left( \frac{{{\text{x}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{2}}+{{\text{x}}_{3}}}{3},\frac{{{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{y}}_{2}}+{{\text{y}}_{3}}}{3} \right)=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ centroid }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

i.e., incentre and centroid coincide in equilateral triangle.

concepts.title: Circumcenter and it's properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Circumcenter of a triangle

The circumcenter of a triangle is the point of intersection of the perpendicular bisectors of the sides of a triangle (i.e., the lines through the midpoint of a side and perpendicular to it).

The co-ordinates are

$\left( \frac{{{\text{x}}_{1}}\text{sin}2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}+{{\text{x}}_{2}}\text{sin}2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}+{{\text{x}}_{3}}\text{sin}2\text{C}}{\text{sin}2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}+\text{sin}2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}+\text{sin}2\text{C}},\frac{{{\text{y}}_{1}}\text{sin}2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}+{{\text{y}}_{2}}\text{sin}2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}+{{\text{y}}_{3}}\text{sin}2\text{C}}{\text{sin}2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ A}+\text{sin}2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}+\text{sin}2\text{C}} \right)$

Or $\left( \frac{a{{x}_{1}}\text{cos}A+b{{x}_{2}}\text{cos}B+c{{x}_{3}}\text{cos}C}{a\text{cos}A+b\text{cos}B+c\text{cos}C},\frac{a{{y}_{1}}\text{cos}A+b{{y}_{2}}\text{cos}B+c{{y}_{3}}\text{cos}C}{a\text{cos}A+b\text{cos}B+c\text{cos}C} \right)$

where $\left| \text{BC} \right|=\text{a},\left| \text{CA} \right|=\text{b}$ and $\left| \text{AB} \right|=\text{c}$

concepts.title: Orthocenter and it's properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Orthocenter of a triangle

The orthocenter of a triangle is the point of intersection of altitudes (i.e., the lines through the vertices and perpendicular to opposite sides). Its co-ordinates are

$\left( \frac{{{x}_{1}}\text{tan}A+{{x}_{2}}\text{tan}B+{{x}_{3}}\text{tan}C}{\text{tan}A+\text{tan}B+\text{tan}C},\frac{{{y}_{1}}\text{tan}A+{{y}_{2}}\text{tan}B+{{y}_{3}}\text{tan}C}{\text{tan}A+\text{tan}B+\text{tan}C} \right)$

Or $\left( \frac{a{{x}_{1}}\text{sec}A+b{{x}_{2}}\text{sec}B+c{{x}_{3}}\text{sec}C}{a\text{sec}A+b\text{sec}B+c\text{sec}C},\frac{a{{y}_{1}}\text{sec}A+b{{y}_{2}}\text{sec}B+c{{y}_{3}}\text{sec}C}{a\text{sec}A+b\text{sec}B+c\text{sec}C} \right)$

where $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left| \text{BC} \right|=\text{a},\left| \text{CA} \right|=\text{b}$ and $\left| \text{AB} \right|=\text{c}$

1.The orthocenter, the centroid and the circumcenter therefore all lie on a straight line.

2. If $\text{O}$ is orthocenter, $\text{G}$ is centroid and $\text{C}$ is circumcenter then to remember it see OGC G divides $\text{O}$ and $\text{C}$ in the ratio $2:1$ (internally).

Note: 

  1. The distance between the orthocenter and circumcenter in an equilateral triangle is zero.
  2. The orthocenter of a triangle having vertices $\left( \alpha ,\beta \right),\left( \beta ,\alpha \right)$ and $\left( \alpha ,\alpha \right)$ is $\left( \alpha ,\alpha \right)$.
  3. If the circumcenter and centroid of a triangle are respectively $\left( \alpha ,\beta \right),\left( \gamma ,\delta \right)$ then orthocenter will be $\left( 3\gamma -2\alpha ,3\delta -2\beta \right)$.

concepts.title: Shifting and rotation of axis
concepts.conceptBody:
Shifting of axes

Translation of Coordinate Axes 

When origin is shifted to a new position $\text{A}\left( \text{h},\text{k} \right)$ without changing the direction of the axes, then coordinates of all points in the plane are obtained by the formulae:

$\text{x}=\mathbf{X}+\text{h }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\mathbf{Y}+\text{k}$ ,where, $\text{P}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ referred to ox, oy and $\text{P}\left( \text{X},\text{Y} \right)$ referred to $\text{AX},\text{AY}$

Note: 

  1. In this case axes are shifted parallel to themselves then it is also called Transformation by parallel axes. 
  2. Inverse translation or shifting the origin back: Sometimes it is required to shift the new origin back. Then putting $\text{x}-\text{h}$ in place of $\text{x}$ and $\text{y}-\text{k}$ in place of $\text{y}$ in any equation of curve referred to the new origin to get the corresponding equation referred to the old origin.
  3. The above transformation is true whether the axes be rectangular or oblique.

Q) The equation of a curve referred to the new axes, axes retaining their direction and origin is $\left( 4,5 \right)$ is ${{x}^{2}}+{{y}^{2}}=36$. Find the equation referred to the original axes. 

Solution: 

Here we want to shift the $\left( 4,5 \right)$ to the origin without changing the direction of axes. Then we replace $\text{x}$ by $\text{x}-4$ and $\text{y}$ by $\text{y}-5$ in the equation of given curve then the required equation is

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {{(\text{x}-4)}^{2}}+{{(\text{y}-5)}^{2}}=36 \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{x}}^{2}}+{{\text{y}}^{2}}-8\text{x}-10\text{y}+5=0 \\ \end{array}$

Q) Shift the origin to a suitable point so that the equation ${{y}^{2}}+4y+8x-2=0$ will not contain term in $y$ and the constant. 

Solution: 

Let the origin be shifted to the point $\left( h,k \right)$ without changing the direction of axes. Then we replace $\text{x}$ by $\text{x}+\text{h}$ and $\text{y}$ by $\text{y}+\text{k}$ in the equation of the given curve then the transformed equation is

$\begin{array}{l} {{(\text{y}+\text{k})}^{2}}+4\left( \text{y}+\text{k} \right)+8\left( \text{x}+\text{h} \right)-2=0 \\ \Rightarrow {{\text{y}}^{2}}+\left( 2\text{k}+4 \right)\text{y}+8\text{x}+\left( {{\text{k}}^{2}}+4\text{k}+8\text{ h}-2 \right)=0 \\ \end{array}$

Since this equation is required to be free from the term containing y and the constant, we have

$\begin{array}{l} {} & 2\text{x}+4=0\text{ and } {{\text{k}}^{2}}+4\text{k}+8\text{ h}-2=0 \\ \therefore & {} \text{k}=-2\text{ and }\text{ h}=\frac{3}{4} \\ \end{array}$

Hence, the point to which the origin be shifted is $\left( \frac{3}{4},-2 \right)$

Rotation of coordinate axes

When coordinate axes are rotated in anti-clockwise direction through an angle $\theta $, about the origin, then coordinates of a point $\text{P}$ in the plane are obtained by the formulae.

$\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{X}=\text{Ycos}\theta -Y\text{sin}\theta \\ \text{y}=\text{Xsin}\theta +Y\text{cos}\theta \\ \end{array} \right.\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & X=x\text{cos}\theta +y\text{sin}\theta \\ {} & Y=y\text{cos}\theta -\text{xsin}\theta \\ \end{array}$

Where $\text{P}$ is $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ referred to coordinate system oxy and $\text{P}$ is $\left( \text{X},\text{Y} \right)$ referred to new coordinates system oXY.

For remembrance 

Q) Prove that if the axes be turned through $\frac{\pi }{4}$ the equation ${{x}^{2}}-{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ is transformed to the form $xy=\lambda $. Find the value of $\lambda $. 

Solution: 

Here $\theta =\frac{\pi }{4}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ so }\!\!~\!\!\text{ sin }\theta =\text{cos}\theta =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

Replacing $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ by $\left( \text{xcos}\theta -\text{ysin}\theta ,\text{xsin}\theta +\text{ycos}\theta \right)$

i.e., $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \frac{\text{x}-\text{y}}{\sqrt{2}},\frac{\text{x}+\text{y}}{\sqrt{2}} \right)$

then ${{x}^{2}}-{{y}^{2}}={{a}^{2}}$ becomes

$\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2-\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2=\mathrm{a}^2$

$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=\mathrm{a}^2$

$\Rightarrow$ $\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(\frac{-2 \mathrm{y}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=\mathrm{a}^2$

or $x y=-\frac{a^2}{2}$

Comparing it with $\mathrm{xy}=\lambda$ then we get $\lambda=-\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{2}$

Q) Through what angle should the axes be rotated so that the equation -$9{{x}^{2}}-2\sqrt{3}xy+7{{y}^{2}}=10$ may be changed to $3{{x}^{2}}+5{{y}^{2}}=5$ ? 

Solution: 

Let angle be $\theta $ then $\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ by $\left( \text{xcos}\theta -\text{ysin}\theta ,\text{xsin}\theta +\text{ycos}\theta \right)$

then $9{{x}^{2}}-2\sqrt{3}xy+7{{y}^{2}}=10$ becomes

$9(x \cos \theta-y \sin \theta)^2-2 \sqrt{3}(x \cos \theta-y \sin \theta)(x \sin \theta+y \cos \theta)+7(x \sin \theta+y \cos \theta)^2=10$

$\Rightarrow \quad x^2\left(9 \cos ^2 \theta-2 \sqrt{3} \sin \theta \cos \theta+7 \sin ^2 \theta\right)$

$+2 x y(-9 \sin \theta \cos \theta-\sqrt{3} \cos 2 \theta+7 \sin \theta \cos \theta)$

$+y^2\left(9 \cos ^2 \theta+2 \sqrt{3} \sin \theta \cos \theta+7 \cos ^2 \theta\right)=10$

On comparing with $3{{\text{x}}^{2}}+5{{\text{y}}^{2}}=5$ (coefficient of $\text{xy}$ )

We get $-9 \sin \theta \cos \theta-\sqrt{3} \cos 2 \theta+7 \sin \theta \cos \theta=0$

or $\sin 2 \theta=-\sqrt{3} \cos 2 \theta$

or $\tan 2 \theta=-\sqrt{3}=\tan (180^{\circ}-60^{\circ})$

or $2 \theta=120^{\circ}$

$\theta=60^{\circ}$

examples.title: Find the value of $m$ if the points $(5,1),(-2,-3)$ and $(8,2 m)$ are collinear.
Find the area of a triangle whose vertices are $(1,-1),(-4,6)$ and $(-3,-5)$.
Find the value of $a$, if the distance between the points $P(-3,-14)$ and $Q(a,-5)$ is 9
If the mid-point of the line segment joining the points $A(3,4)$ and $B(k, 6)$ is $P(x, y)$ and $x+y-10=0$, find the value of $k$.
